ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for packaged winter melon drink; mapped to broader beverage food product class. Winter melon tea / dong gua cha is a traditional Chinese herbal-style beverage not individually coded in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code for winter melon drink; A0FJP (sugar-sweetened beverages) is the closest defensible category match given the product's sugar content and RTD format.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for packaged winter melon drink as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve portion is one can or bottle of approximately 250 ml, providing around 60 kcal and 15 g of carbohydrate including approximately 11 g of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Winter Melon Drink — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for packaged winter melon drink (cr_id D05010044). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sugar-sweetened beverages and soft drinks — analogous estimate based on sucrose-containing RTD beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for packaged winter melon drink found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 63 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: sucrose-sweetened ready-to-drink beverages with similar sugar content (~4–6 g/100 ml), which consistently yield GI values in the 58–65 range in the Sydney database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (63) × carbohydrate per serving (15 g) / 100 = 9.45, rounded to 9.5. Serving size used: 250 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (4.48 g/100 ml) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB); regular SSB consumption is associated with weight gain and metabolic dysfunction that elevates blood pressure, despite the very low sodium content of 1 mg/100 ml.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should avoid regular consumption of packaged sugar-sweetened beverages including this product, as the AHA recommends limiting added sugar intake to reduce cardiovascular and blood pressure risk; replace with plain water or unsweetened herbal tea.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: This is a sugar-sweetened beverage with 4.48 g of sugar per 100 ml and zero dietary fibre, resulting in rapid glucose absorption and acute postprandial glycaemic excursion; the ADA classifies all SSBs as unsuitable for Type 2 Diabetes management regardless of absolute sugar quantity.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this packaged winter melon drink entirely, as liquid sugar without fibre causes rapid blood glucose elevation; choose plain water, unsweetened winter melon tea brewed without sugar, or other zero-sugar beverages instead.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased risk of cardiovascular events, dyslipidaemia, and visceral adiposity; this product contains added sugar with no protective nutrients (zero fibre, zero omega-3, negligible micronutrients).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit or eliminate packaged sugar-sweetened beverages from their diet in line with AHA dietary guidelines, and replace this product with unsweetened beverages to reduce added sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Packaged winter melon drink (冬瓜饮料) is a ready-to-drink (RTD) sugar-sweetened beverage widely sold in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. It is made from water, winter melon (Benincasa hispida) extract or concentrate, and added sugar, then commercially packaged in cans or bottles. The drink has a mild, lightly sweet flavour with subtle herbal and earthy notes characteristic of winter melon. It is typically served chilled and consumed as a refreshing beverage, particularly in hot weather. Nutritionally, it provides 24 kcal per 100 ml, with 6 g of carbohydrate (4.48 g sugar) and negligible protein, fat, and dietary fibre. Sodium content is very low at 1 mg/100 ml. Despite its low calorie count, it is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) because it contains added sugar with zero fibre, which causes rapid glucose absorption. It is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension due to its SSB classification per ADA and AHA guidelines. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 63 (medium) based on analogous sucrose-sweetened RTD beverages. A typical serving of 250 ml provides approximately 60 kcal and 15 g of carbohydrate. The product is vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal-suitable. It is commonly consumed as a casual drink, at hawker centres, or as a packaged convenience beverage in Singapore, Malaysia, and other parts of Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics drink winter melon drink?
How many calories in a can of winter melon drink?
Is packaged winter melon drink suitable for hypertension?
Is winter melon drink high in sugar?
Glycemic index of winter melon drink
Is winter melon drink good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D05010044) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The food is a commercially standardised packaged product with consistent formulation. GI is estimated from analogous sucrose-sweetened RTD beverages in the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the only approved GI source; this introduces a minor uncertainty captured in the gi_source notes.
notes: Zero-macro justification: protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo — this is a water-based sugar-sweetened beverage with no protein-containing ingredients. fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo — the product contains no fat-contributing ingredients by formulation. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero by formulation. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB — the product is a clarified liquid with no intact plant fibre. Negative sentinel values: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profiles for this packaged beverage are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for D05010044 as of 2026. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 per HPB source. GI note: No direct GI measurement for packaged winter melon drink was found in the University of Sydney GI Database; GI of 63 is an analogous estimate based on sucrose-sweetened RTD beverages of similar sugar concentration from the Sydney database. Multilingual note: Malay name uses 'tembikai susu' (wax gourd / winter melon) as the closest standard Malay term; 'dong gua' is the common Chinese name. Tamil and Hindi names are transliterations of the English description as no standardised culinary term exists in those languages for this specific packaged product.
